questions.question: Define an embedded system and list its four key characteristics.
keyPoints: Definition: specialized computing system for dedicated functions
Characteristics: dedicated functionality, real-time operation, low power consumption, cost-effective
Additional points: reliability, minimal user interface, deterministic behavior

questions.question: Explain the difference between a microcontroller and a microprocessor with a suitable diagram.
keyPoints: Microprocessor: CPU only, requires external memory and I/O
Microcontroller: CPU + memory + I/O on single chip
Diagram showing microprocessor with external components vs microcontroller as self-contained unit
Advantages of each approach

questions.question: Classify embedded systems based on performance and complexity. Give one example for each category.
keyPoints: Small-scale: 8-bit processor, simple functions (e.g., microwave oven)
Medium-scale: 16-bit processor, moderate complexity (e.g., printers)
Sophisticated: 32-bit/64-bit processor, complex OS (e.g., smartphones)

questions.question: What is the difference between hard real-time and soft real-time embedded systems? Give one example for each.
keyPoints: Hard real-time: missing deadline = system failure, strict timing constraints
Soft real-time: missing deadline degrades performance but not fatal
Example hard: airbag control, flight control system
Example soft: video streaming, audio player

questions.question: List the main components of embedded system hardware and explain the function of any two.
keyPoints: Components: Processor, Memory, Input devices, Output devices
Processor: executes instructions, controls all operations
Memory: stores program and data (volatile/non-volatile)
Input: sensors, keypads - interface with external world
Output: displays, actuators - provide results to user

questions.question: What is a System-on-Chip (SoC)? Explain its advantages over traditional embedded system design.
keyPoints: SoC: integrates all components on single silicon chip
Advantages: smaller size, lower power, reduced cost, higher reliability
Components integrated: CPU, memory, I/O, sometimes radio/wireless
Used in: mobile phones, IoT devices, wearables

questions.question: Explain the role of ADC and DAC in embedded systems with practical applications.
keyPoints: ADC: converts analog sensor signals to digital for processor
DAC: converts digital processor output to analog for actuators
ADC application: temperature sensor reading, audio input
DAC application: motor speed control, audio output
Essential for interfacing with real-world analog devices

questions.question: Draw and explain the detailed hardware architecture of an embedded system with block diagram. Discuss the function of each block.
keyPoints: Block diagram showing: Processor (CPU), Memory (ROM/RAM), Input Unit (sensors, ADC), Output Unit (actuators, DAC, displays), Communication Interfaces, Power Supply, Clock circuit
Processor: brain - fetches, decodes, executes instructions
Memory: ROM for firmware, RAM for temporary data
Input: sensors convert physical to electrical, ADC for digitization
Output: DAC for analog control, drivers for actuators, displays for output
Communication: UART, I2C, SPI for peripheral connection
Power: voltage regulation, battery management
Clock: provides timing signals for synchronization

questions.question: Explain the classification of embedded systems based on: (a) performance, (b) complexity, and (c) functional requirements. Give suitable examples for each classification.
keyPoints: (a) Performance: Small-scale (8-bit, simple), Medium-scale (16-bit), Sophisticated (32/64-bit)
(b) Complexity: Standalone, Real-time, Networked
(c) Function: Single-function (washing machine), Multi-function (smartphone), Reactive (airbag)
Examples with justification for each category
Comparison table showing parameters

questions.question: Discuss the important characteristics of embedded systems that distinguish them from general-purpose computers. How do these characteristics influence system design?
keyPoints: Key characteristics: dedicated functionality, real-time constraints, low power, cost-effective, reliable, minimal UI
Dedicated: optimized for specific task, no unnecessary features
Real-time: deterministic behavior, deadline handling
Low power: battery operation, power management techniques
Cost-effective: minimize hardware, use appropriate processor
Reliability: fault tolerance, watchdog timers, self-diagnostics
Design impact: hardware-software co-design, platform selection, optimization priorities

questions.question: Explain the various types of memory used in embedded systems with their characteristics and applications.
keyPoints: Volatile: RAM - fast, loses data on power off, for temporary storage
Non-volatile: ROM - factory programmed, read-only
Flash: rewritable non-volatile, for firmware storage
EEPROM: electrically erasable, for parameter storage
Cache: fast memory between CPU and RAM
Selection criteria: speed, cost, durability, write cycles
Trade-offs between different memory types
